mnemonics.title: Construction Order: Build Upward, Tear Downward
mnemonics.content: Remember: Constructors build from base to derived (bottom-up), destructors destroy from derived to base (top-down). Like building a house: lay foundation first, then walls, then roof. When demolishing: tear down roof first, then walls, then foundation.

mnemonics.title: Global/Static/Local Memory Trick
mnemonics.content: G-L-S-S: Global (before main), Local (when block enters), Static (first time/program end). Global: Starts with program. Static: First use or program end. Local: Block entry. Dynamic: delete.

mnemonics.title: Virtual Destructor Always Needed
mnemonics.content: When in doubt, make destructor virtual. VIRTUE: Virtual In Runtime To Undo Everything - ensures proper cleanup for polymorphic deletions.

acronyms.usage: Constructor acquires resources, destructor releases them. Ensures proper resource management tied to object lifetime.
Containers like vector call constructors/destructors when inserting/removing elements.
File handle in constructor, close in destructor; lock in constructor, unlock in destructor.

visualTips.visualization: Draw a staircase: Base at bottom, Derived above. Constructor walks UP the stairs (Base→Derived). Destructor walks DOWN the stairs (Derived→Base). Label steps with '1, 2, 3' for constructors and '3, 2, 1' for destructors.

Draw horizontal timeline: Program Start | Global objects | main() begins | Function call | Local object | Function return | Local destroyed | main() ends | Global destroyed | Program End. Mark 'Constructor' and 'Destructor' events at each point.
Draw stack growing downward. When function called: push stack frame with local objects (constructors run). When function returns: pop frame (destructors run in reverse order). Visualize LIFO cleanup.

analogies.concept: Constructor as Birth, Destructor as Death
analogies.analogy: Like humans: Constructor is born (resources allocated), Destructor dies (resources released). Just as we can't be 'half-born', objects must be fully constructed before use, and properly 'buried' (destructed) after use.
Inheritance construction is like genealogy: Grandparent (base) must exist before parent, parent before child. Destruction: Child dies before parent, parent before grandparent.
Virtual destructor is like a master key that works for all derived locks. Without it, the base lock (destructor) opens but derived room contents remain locked inside.
